title: Layers of Eva
wikitext:
[]: Layers of Eva             Quote Post by Shinji-kun » Fri Aug 07, 2009 8:35 pm Eva is so complex that it requires years of study to understand. I was wondering if we could come up with a list of the different layers of Eva to make it easier to study and research. Perhaps list things for each category. Ie -plot -visial meaning/narrative -technical details -psychology -themes/messages Jimbo?

[]:             Quote Post by Agent_Koopa » Fri Aug 07, 2009 8:49 pm I think "years of study" is kind of pushing it.

[]:             Quote Post by Shinji-kun » Fri Aug 07, 2009 8:52 pm Eva Yojimbo studied it for 2.5 years and Evamonkey has been a fan for 10 including study.

[]:             Quote Post by wonderluster » Fri Aug 07, 2009 8:54 pm When I saw this thread I thought it was being literal...Now Im disapointed......

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Aug 07, 2009 9:34 pm The layers overlap, but I think perhaps the best division might be: -Story/Plot -Characters -Themes -Visual, Cinematic, & Narrative craft All of these things, of course, are interrelated. NGE is very much a holistic show, but if we are going to break it down I think this is probably the best way. Why? Because each one of these elements can be analyzed on their own. The characters are creations which function in a plot/story, the relation of the characters and the plot/story relates the themes of the series, the craft is how that relationship is handled and delivered. Obviously, NGE, to paraphrase a criticism of Vertigo, is a perfect organism with each character, each sequence, the plot and chracters, craft and form, illuminating each other. While analyzing the work organicistically can work, the work's true profundity comes through its "greater than the sum of its parts" holism. That said, almost each of these elements can be broken up into sub-sections. Themes, especially, since NGE has an incredibly broad range of themes ranging from the socially critical to the psychology and philosophy. Agent_Koopa wrote: I think "years of study" is kind of pushing it.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Fri Aug 07, 2009 9:49 pm That's tough. I don't think Evangelion is simple enough to be compartmentalized like this--yeah, it reeks of deconstructionism, but to divide it down like you're scraping paint off a wall isn't good enough of an analogy. Like any really well-done piece of artwork, NGE uses each of its components in tandem--to try and differentiate between the components as being separate is meaningless. They're all reliant upon each other. The 'visual meaning', 'technical details', 'psychology', and 'themes and messages' are all one in the same, with the 'technical details' and the 'narrative' working as a bridge between that stuff and the plot. NGE is sophisticated, but I look at it more as a fluid thing--you dive into it, because there are no real layers of examination--just different aspects of itself that can be analyzed and interpreted. Maybe I'm confused about what you're asking for. Are you looking for just analyses of these different aspects? How the cinematography, for instance, is key to understanding the nuances of the plot, or how much of the psychological ideas are portrayed by different recurring motifs and symbols throughout the show--that kind of thing? If that were the case, I think it’d be easiest to analyze one aspect within the context of another aspect. Or maybe I'm just missing the point entirely because I'm over thinking the subject. EDIT Christ, I took to long. Oh well.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Aug 07, 2009 10:00 pm I get the fluidity you're talking about Merridian, but no (traditional) work is free from being made of parts and aspects which can be analyzed individually, even if those parts and aspects all work together to bring about something greater than the mere sum of those parts. I think the thing is that most of the best works of (let's limit it to "narrative") art is that it becomes much harder to extricate the parts from the whole and analyze them individually since they all interact. I've always had great difficulty analyzing a single aspect of NGE without essentially veering off into other aspects to explain how they support/reinforce each other. Like how in the "FoK = DNA" thread I can't talk about it just in terms of NGE's narrative without bringing into play the interconnected themes of the Genesis allusion and Jungian interpretation of consciousness/unconsciousness, and self-discovery/realization; they're all inextricably linked. But that doesn't mean one can't try. For instance, you can analyze a character, specifically, and see how their actions in the narrative characterize their personality and psychology, or you can look to the visual craft to see how it lends clues. Put another way, it is possible to analyze the parts, but you must use the "light" of the other aspects to illuminate them so they can be analyzed.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Fri Aug 07, 2009 10:55 pm Right right, but what I was trying to say was that you still need to contextualize everything you analyze--in other words, Hamlet's character only makes sense within the play of Hamlet ; one cannot remove him from the context, since his character is equally an entity to be analyzed as it is a piece in a much larger work (the play). I think that's the Holistic aspect you were looking at. Actually, I don't think that was what I was trying to say at all, but it's still an important thing to keep in mind (I've been slightly under the weather, so I'll be hittin' da sack soon; sorry if this is incoherent). I guess what I was trying to get at was that it's easier to analyze different thematic aspects by using technical and plot aspects as lenses though which to frame them. Basically--well, to totally rip from another thread--how different aspects of 'motherhood' and 'divinity' are played off each other through the character of Rei; that kind of idea is best dissected by means of HOW Anno & Co. portrayed Rei throughout the series. It goes without saying, but it's still important to keep in mind that the characters of Eva were as dependent upon the plot as the plot was dependent upon their characterizations, since NGE is a wonderful example of a plot AND character driven story. So, while one can comment on how awesome Anno framed a scene, it doesn't have any validity unless one explains WHY Anno framed the scene the way he did (on the technical end of things). Speaking of the technical side of everything, it is the technical aspect that's probably the most important aspect of NGE (or any art) anyhow, since THAT's the thing bringing everything to the viewer. So any way you look at NGE, it'd be easiest to use the technical aspects as a context from which you could branch out into the thematic and plot content--and then, if you were feeling ambitious enough (which, since we're all part of EvaGeeks, I'm sure all of us are), the intuitive thing to do would be to connect these thematic/plot branches with each other, while still using the technical aspect as a base of operations. I look at analysis as a tree-ish looking thing; if I'm presented with a chunk of artwork (especially literature and cinema, but also music and to a lesser extent visual artwork--but only because I'm not as trained in that kind of critique as I am in the rest), I look at as though it's a body of water of a plot of soil, and I'm this plant. The roots of this plant are my thought patterns as I read/watch/listen and otherwise experience this work, and the branches topside are essentially the conclusions I come to. The deeper I extend roots into this thing, the more I'll get out of it, and the ultimate goal is to be able to tie up all of my roots within each other so that all of my thought patterns connect and interrelate with each other. The branches will continue to grow as well, since the conclusions I come to will also be reanalyzed as often as I revisit the work. But all the while, the soil is just the easiest way that the minerals are presented—the medium, cinema in NGE’s case, is this soil. The best way to retrieve the nutrients is to understand how the medium works, and that’s what the technical side of things is all about. That’s really the whole basis of any critique anyway. OK at this point I’m running in circles. Time for sleep. I hope I got my point across. Eva Yojimbo wrote: Put another way, it is possible to analyze the parts, but you must use the "light" of the other aspects to illuminate them so they can be analyzed.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Aug 08, 2009 1:11 am Merridian wrote: Hamlet's character only makes sense within the play of Hamlet ; one cannot remove him from the context, since his character is equally an entity to be analyzed as it is a piece in a much larger work (the play).

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Sat Aug 08, 2009 10:55 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Well, that's a long standing debate: Content or form?

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Aug 09, 2009 9:52 am I don't inherently disagree with you, it's simply that, in my experience, the vast majority of people don't give a damn about form. All they look at is content, especially story and characters, and forget about everything else... well, they might ponder the themes if the themes are made important or if something in the content connects with the viewer. I've often vaguely pondered on various ideas related to this: "If form is meant to facilitate and augment form, but most people are ignorant of form and only focus on content, can the form have any actual impact on the audience?" One way to think about this is that most people only intellectually (consciously) process a small amount of any given work the first time through, and they generally form opinions based on a haphazard group of impressions and how those impressions relate to various other mental content, information, context, expectations, etc. When this happens, is it even possible for the relatively subtle use of a form that's primarily ignored to have any kind of impact? In many ways it seems that things like film theory are there to critique how these unconscious techniques have an effect on an unconscious audience, yet I think it's almost impossible to really say. Someone not familiar with montage theory simply isn't going to get something out of Eisenstein's intellectual montages, for instance. Anyway, I dunno. I generally agree that form is perhaps the primary thing we should analyze because if something is worth analyzing then the content usually will relate to it strongly. It often seems that the best works in any medium are those which can ONLY work (or can only work as effectively they do) in that medium, and this usually speaks to the unique and inherent potential of artistic mediums to communicate things in a method others can't.

[]:             Quote Post by VoidEater » Mon Aug 10, 2009 3:31 pm There is little true McLuhan-ism in the world. Deconstructing art is always de-synergistic - but is still useful since understanding a facet leads to a greater understanding of the whole. I like Jimbo's categories at the top of a heirarchy.
